title: Quiz: Introduction to Risk Measures
description: Test your understanding of fundamental financial risk measures

questions.question: What is the most common measure of investment risk?
questions.explanation: Standard deviation, also known as volatility, is the most common measure of investment risk. It quantifies the dispersion of returns around the mean.

questions.question: What is a major limitation of using standard deviation as a risk measure?

options.text: It treats upside and downside movements equally
It can only be calculated annually
It requires at least 1000 data points

questions.explanation: Standard deviation treats upside gains and downside losses equally, even though investors typically care more about downside risk.

questions.question: What does maximum drawdown measure?

options.text: The largest peak-to-trough decline before a new peak
The total number of losing days

questions.explanation: Maximum drawdown is the largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value before a new peak is reached, measuring the worst-case loss an investor could have experienced.

questions.question: Which risk-adjusted return metric uses downside deviation instead of standard deviation?
questions.explanation: The Sortino ratio uses downside deviation in the denominator instead of total standard deviation, focusing only on harmful volatility.

questions.question: Standard deviation is a coherent risk measure.
questions.explanation: False. Standard deviation is not coherent because it doesn't satisfy monotonicity - it treats both positive and negative deviations from the mean equally.

questions.question: What does sub-additivity mean for a risk measure?

options.text: Diversification reduces or maintains portfolio risk

questions.explanation: Sub-additivity means ρ(X+Y) ≤ ρ(X) + ρ(Y), implying that combining positions doesn't increase total risk beyond the sum of individual risks - diversification benefits.

questions.question: The Calmar ratio divides annualized return by which risk measure?
questions.explanation: The Calmar ratio is calculated as annualized return divided by the absolute value of maximum drawdown.

questions.question: Which are properties of coherent risk measures? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Coherent risk measures satisfy four properties: monotonicity, sub-additivity, positive homogeneity, and translation invariance. They don't require normality assumptions.
